title: Sleep Study -- Interfering Factors
content: Despite being the gold standard for diagnosing multiple sleep disorders, polysomnograms (PSGs) have several limitations. For example, the first-night effect, which is due to poor sleep because of a new sleeping environment, may lead to underestimating OSA due to the potential for decreased REM sleep being captured. [22] Medication changes before a PSG may also interfere with the quality and quantity of sleep during a PSG. Additionally, some sleep disorders such as nocturnal seizures and RBD may occur too infrequently to be captured during a single night study reliably. Equipment issues can contribute to PSG inaccuracies, given the many physiologic recordings and potential for electrodes and instrument cable malfunction.

title: Types of Variables and Commonly Used Statistical Designs -- Issues of Concern -- Independent Samples T-test
content: An independent samples t-test allows a comparison of two groups of subjects on one (continuous) variable. Examples in biomedical research include comparing results of treatment vs. control group and comparing differences based on gender (male vs. female). Example: Does adherence to the ketogenic diet (yes/no; two groups) have a differential effect on total sleep time (minutes; continuous)?

title: REM Rebound Effect -- Differential Diagnosis
content: OSA leads to poor sleep quality in patients. The gold standard for diagnosing OSA is overnight PSG, and the most effective treatment is overnight CPAP. [20] A meta-analysis comparing OSA treatments shows no significant difference in outcomes between CPAP and bilevel-PAP. Nevertheless, treatment compliance remains a significant challenge for all OSA treatments, with the exception of surgical interventions. REM sleep rebound following initial OSA treatment is associated with improved sleep quality and elevated mood. Please see StatPearls' companion resource, " Obstructive Sleep Apnea ," for more information. [21]

title: Sleep Study -- Interfering Factors
content: In addition to diagnostic limitations, there are also systemic interfering factors with PSG. Performing a PSG requires an adequate sleep period, highly trained technicians to administer the study, and sleep providers interpreting the study, resulting in high cost. As a result, insurance companies usually require prior authorizations, placing many barriers between the patient and their ability to get a PSG even when indicated. The PSG data itself is also not portable in many cases, requiring unique software that is not readily exportable.

title: Sleep and Neurodegenerative Disorders -- Epidemiology
content: The coexistence of SDB and Parkinson disease has been reported in the literature in variable frequency (prevalence between 27% and 73%). [31] [32] Even though several studies have indicated that many patients with Parkinson disease experience SDB, the reported prevalence varies due to various factors, such as different definitions of respiratory events, severity thresholds of the disease, or diagnostic methods employed. [33] [34] This variability in reported prevalence is explained by heterogeneity in sample size, disease severity, and diagnostic definition and testing modality. For example, using standard diagnostic polysomnography (PSG) in a retrospective series of 49 patients with Parkinson disease, at least 21 patients (43%) had OSA diagnosed (apnea/hypopnea index [AHI] ≥5 events/hour). [35]

content: Apnea (الاِختِنَاق) is the overarching respiratory disturbance that encompasses the broader category of respiratory disorder (اضطراب تَنَفُّسِيّ). Within this category, the clinical condition described as respiratory failure; insufficiency; arrest (adult) represents a more severe manifestation. Thus, adult respiratory failure, insufficiency, and arrest are subtypes of the respiratory disorder, which itself is nested under the primary concept of apnea, illustrating a hierarchical relationship that links a fundamental breathing abnormality to increasingly critical respiratory pathologies.

title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 47
content: Depression (excluding suicide and self‑harm) (SMQ) is presented as a broad clinical category that encompasses multiple depressive subtypes—including reactive depression, endogenous depression, melancholic depression, postpartum depression, and tolerance—as well as distinct sleep‑wake disturbances such as transient disorders of awakening initiation or maintenance and persistent disorders of awakening initiation or maintenance. The SMQ also subsumes a wide range of associated phenomena and comorbid conditions, including emotional poverty, indifference, heightened vigilance, alcohol poisoning, marijuana use, mood swings, attention disturbances, memory disturbances, psychological distress, poor concentration, motor hyperactivity, muscular hyperactivity, and excessive daytime sleepiness. Within this framework, loss of ambition is classified under depression, indicating that diminished motivation may manifest as depressive symptomatology. Non‑infectious encephalitis (SMQ) incorporates both the transient and persistent disorders of awakening initiation or maintenance, linking sleep‑wake regulation abnormalities to an encephalopathic condition. This SMQ further includes hyperkinetic motor activity, hyperactive muscle tone, and primary excessive daytime sleepiness, highlighting a spectrum of motor and arousal disturbances. Apathy is identified as a component of delirium/non‑infectious encephalopathy (SMQ), connecting reduced motivation with acute brain dysfunction. Delirium/non‑infectious encephalopathy also comprises attention disorder, reduced concentration, and primary excessive daytime sleepiness, underscoring the cognitive, attentional, and vigilance impairments that frequently accompany this syndrome. Together, these relationships illustrate the heterogeneous nature of depressive disorders and their overlap with sleep‑wake, motor, and encephalopathic abnormalities, guiding clinicians toward comprehensive assessment and tailored treatment strategies.

Likewise, “اللامبالاة” is a component of the delirium/brain‑damage category “الهذيان/الاعتلال الدماغي غير المعدي (SMQ),” which also comprises attention deficits, reduced concentration, and excessive daytime sleepiness. Collectively, these interrelated entities depict a heterogeneous network in which depressive, psychotic, substance‑use, encephalitic, and delirium‑related conditions share overlapping clinical features—such as reduced affect, hyperactivity, sleep‑wake disturbances, and cognitive impairments—underscoring the necessity for integrated assessment and personalized management in clinical practice.

title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 0 -- Community 2
content: “non‑infectious encephalitis” SMQ, connecting sleep‑wake dysregulation to inflammatory brain processes. Apathy belongs to the “non‑infectious delirium/brain disease” SMQ, which additionally includes attention‑deficit, concentration weakness and early daytime sleepiness, linking cognitive‑motor deficits with delirium‑type brain disease. Gender identity is associated with stereotyped repetitive movements; together with emotional blunting, these signs are interpreted as clinical expressions of localized brain atrophy. Miscellaneous mental‑health disorders are broadly related to an unspecified transient organic mental disorder and are primarily mapped from organic mood disorder, indicating an organic substrate for diverse psychiatric presentations. A confusional state without acute features and an organic hallucination syndrome are members of the “anticholinergic syndrome” SMQ, reflecting anticholinergic toxicity as a cause of acute psychotic‑like states. title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 1 -- Community 214
content: and maintenance. Substance‑use disorders (اضطراب تعاطي المواد الإدمانية) contain alcohol‑related disorders as a subset, and both are associated with the depressive spectrum and with behavioral dysregulation. The behavior disorder category also comprises non‑inflammatory brain disease (التهاب الدماغ غير المعدي (SMQ)), schizophrenia and other psychotic disorders (الذهان وال اضطرابات الذهانية (SMQ)), and delirium/non‑inflammatory brain pathology (الهذيان/الاعتلال الدماغي غير المعدي (SMQ)). Within the psychotic disorder group, depressive states (recurrent and single‑episode major depressive episodes, atypical depression, mood lability) frequently co‑occur, and psychotic disorders are linked to blunted affect (عاطفة متقلصة). Non‑inflammatory brain disease is associated with motor hyperactivity, muscular hyperactivity, excessive daytime sleepiness, and both transient and persistent disorders of wake‑initiation or maintenance, highlighting its role in motor and sleep regulation.

title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 144
content: Behavioral/psychiatric manifestations are organized under the broader concept of الهَذَيان (delirium), and الهَذَيان in turn includes the specific category حالات ذُهانية عضوية عابرة (transient organic delirium cases). Both التهاب الدماغ غير المعدي (SMQ) (non‑infectious encephalitis) and الهذيان/الاعتلال الدماغي غير المعدي (SMQ) (delirium/non‑infectious brain disorder) are SMQ groupings that list حالات ذُهانية عضوية عابرة as members. Within التهاب الدماغ غير المعدي (SMQ), the following clinical features are identified as members: الاضطراب العابر في بدء الاستيقاظ أو المحافظة عليه (transient disorder of sleep onset or maintenance), الاضطراب المستمر في بدء الاستيقاظ أو استمراره (continuous disorder of sleep onset or maintenance), فرط النشاط الحركي (hyperkinetic activity), فرط النشاط العضلي (muscle hyperactivity), and فرط النوم في النهار الأولي (primary excessive daytime sleepiness). In the related grouping الهذيان/الاعتلال الدماغي غير المعدي (SMQ), the entities اللامبالاة (apathy), خلل في الانتباه (attention deficit), ضعف التركيز (poor concentration), and فرط النوم في النهار الأولي (primary excessive daytime sleepiness) are listed as members. This hierarchical structure shows how delirium‑related behavioral and psychiatric manifestations are linked to specific transient organic delirium presentations and how those presentations are further categorized under distinct non‑infectious encephalitic and delirium brain‑disorder SMQ clusters, each characterized by particular sleep‑wake disturbances, motor abnormalities, and cognitive‑affective symptoms.
